questions.question: Matter occupies space and has mass.
questions.explanation: This is the basic definition of matter. Anything that occupies space and has mass is called matter.

questions.question: Molecules of a substance are always at rest.
questions.explanation: Molecules are in constant motion. They possess kinetic energy and keep moving continuously.

questions.question: Cohesion is the force of attraction between molecules of different substances.
questions.explanation: Cohesion is the force of attraction between molecules of the SAME substance. The force between molecules of different substances is called adhesion.

questions.question: Gases have neither definite shape nor definite volume.
questions.explanation: In gases, molecules are widely apart with very weak inter-molecular forces, so gases have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume. They take the shape and volume of their container.

questions.question: The melting point of ice is 100°C.
questions.explanation: The melting point of ice is 0°C, not 100°C. 100°C is the boiling point of water.

questions.question: During a change of state, the temperature remains constant even though heat is being supplied.
questions.explanation: During a change of state, heat energy (latent heat) is absorbed or released without any change in temperature. The heat goes into breaking or forming inter-molecular bonds.

questions.question: The melting point of naphthalene is 80°C.
questions.explanation: The melting point of naphthalene is 80°C as stated in the textbook.

questions.question: Water contracts on freezing like most other substances.
questions.explanation: Water is an exception. It EXPANDS on freezing, unlike most substances such as wax and lead which contract on freezing.
questions.source_section: Effect of Pressure on Melting Point

questions.question: The melting point of wax increases with increase in pressure.
questions.explanation: The melting point of wax increases with increase in pressure. This is because wax contracts on freezing (unlike ice which expands on freezing).
questions.source_section: Effect of Pressure on Melting Point

questions.question: Evaporation occurs only at the boiling point of a liquid.
questions.explanation: Evaporation occurs at ALL temperatures, not just at the boiling point. It is a slow process that takes place at the surface of a liquid at any temperature.

questions.question: Camphor is an example of a substance that undergoes sublimation.
questions.explanation: Camphor sublimes, meaning it changes directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid state. Other examples include ammonium chloride, iodine, naphthalene, and dry ice.

questions.question: Adding salt to ice increases its melting point.
questions.explanation: Adding salt to ice DECREASES its melting point. This is the principle used in making freezing mixtures for kulfis.
questions.source_section: Effect of Impurities on Melting Point

questions.question: Higher humidity decreases the rate of evaporation.
questions.explanation: When humidity is high, the air already contains a lot of water vapour, so the rate of evaporation decreases.

questions.question: The boiling point of alcohol is 78.3°C.
questions.explanation: The boiling point of alcohol is 78.3°C, which is lower than the boiling point of water (100°C).

questions.question: In sublimation-prone solids, the inter-molecular forces are very strong.
questions.explanation: In solids that undergo sublimation, the inter-molecular forces are WEAK, which is why they can change directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid state.
